task_summary: Cross-tabulate co-occurrence of financial + non-financial barriers, broken down by community
csv_content:
respondent_id,community,age_band,response_text
R-401,Community-A,45-64,"I just can't get the bus there in time after work — by the time I'd be off shift the surgery's closed. The Saturday clinic is full for weeks ahead. So I've stopped trying."
R-402,Community-B,18-29,"My English is okay but the medical words my GP uses I don't always understand and I'm too embarrassed to ask. I don't want to look stupid."
R-403,Community-A,65+,"After my husband passed I couldn't really afford the prescriptions so I took half doses. Doctor never asked, I never said."
R-404,Community-C,30-44,"It's not the money — we've got coverage. It's that nobody at the practice looks like me or speaks like my grandmother. It feels like a different country in there."
R-405,Community-B,30-44,"I work nights at the warehouse so taking my kid to appointments means I either skip sleep or skip work. Skipping work isn't really a choice."
R-406,Community-A,45-64,"Honestly I just don't trust them. We've been told the wrong thing too many times. I'd rather wait it out and see if it goes away."
R-407,Community-C,18-29,"Same-day appointments don't exist anymore. I called eight times last month. I gave up."
R-408,Community-B,65+,"My daughter usually comes with me to translate but she's at uni now so I haven't been able to go."
R-409,Community-A,30-44,"Between the deductible and the time off I just can't manage it. The transport's there but the cost is the killer."
R-410,Community-C,65+,"I can pay for it but the receptionists are rude every time. I don't go back unless I have to."
opening_prompt: Building on the themes work — what we'd actually like to do next is something a bit more analytical. For each respondent who describes a financial barrier, we're interested in whether they ALSO describe at least one non-financial barrier — the picture we're trying to build is whether financial pressure shows up alone or whether it tends to co-occur with other kinds of constraint. And ideally we'd want to see this broken down by community, because we've a hypothesis that the co-occurrence pattern looks different between Community A and the others. I realise I'm asking quite a lot — does this feel like something we can set up?
probe: Multi-step analytical pipeline: (1) per-row LLM labelling for financial / non-financial, (2) per-row derived 'has both' flag, (3) aggregation by community + co-occurrence flag, (4) summary output. Tests: (1) does the LLM decompose the analytical question into pipeline steps, (2) does it produce a usable summary or just a per-row classification, (3) does it ask the right clarifying questions ('what counts as financial?', 'what's the output shape — counts? proportions?').
product_capability_used: csv source + LLM classify (multi-label binary: has_financial / has_nonfinancial) + value_transform (compute has_both) + batch_stats (group_by community, count) + json sink.
expected_outcome: Real summary output: per community, count of (financial-only, non-financial-only, both, neither). Or at minimum per-row labels with both flags so Sarah can do the group-by herself.
pass_criterion: Either: (a) full analytical pipeline producing the cross-tab (high bar), or (b) per-row labelled output with the right two flags + honest 'you can pivot this in Excel for the cross-tab' close-out (acceptable). Producing only the per-row classification without acknowledging the second part = fail.
